Why are you interested in this role?: What pulls me to this role is that it's the front end of the people function—finding the right person and giving them an experience that makes them want to join. At Kronos Research I owned a tough international search end-to-end, sourced passive talent, and personalized outreach until applications grew 566% in two months. The craft of it—the organization, the follow-through, the relationships—is what I enjoy most. A Recruiter II role lets me do that at the pace and scale of a company I'd be genuinely excited to recruit for.
Why do you want to work at Duolingo?: Honestly, the mission. My degrees are in Psychology and Education Studies and Applied Organizational Psychology—I care about how people learn and grow, and Duolingo making education universally available is something I'd be proud to help build by bringing in great people. Beyond that, the role fits how I work: I treat recruiting as a system to diagnose and improve, partner closely with hiring managers on strategy, and let pipeline data tell me what's working. I run multiple concurrent programs at once today, so juggling several searches without dropping a thread is familiar territory.
How do you use data in your recruiting p: I let the data tell me where the search is actually stuck rather than guessing. At NextGen I analyzed 2,000+ employee experiences and turned the findings into reports that changed strategy, so I'm comfortable working from evidence. In a search, I'd watch pipeline conversion at each stage—where candidates drop, where time-to-fill stretches—and adjust sourcing or the process accordingly, in partnership with the hiring manager. I also use AI tools like ChatGPT daily to speed up sourcing and outreach drafting, and I've been building a RAG-based system in my own time, so I'm genuinely fluent in working AI into a workflow.
Tell us about yourself / anything else: I'm an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, grounded in an MS in Applied Organizational Psychology from USC. My supervisor called me the most organized person he's ever worked with—that organization is what lets me run many parallel searches cleanly. I have hands-on Greenhouse experience and pick up new ATS and sourcing tools fast. I'd love to bring that mix of structure, data, and genuine people-focus to recruiting for a mission I actually care about.
How has AI changed the way you approach: AI has become part of how I work day-to-day, not a side tool. I use it to move faster from messy input to a clear decision—synthesizing stakeholder feedback, drafting and pressure-testing communications, and spotting patterns in data I'd otherwise comb through by hand. What's changed most is my starting point: I now ask what a well-designed AI workflow could take off my plate before I default to doing it manually. I've even been building a RAG-style system for my own job search with a friend, where the AI has access to my background and preferences so it can evaluate fit for me specifically—which made me realize how much the same personalization principle scales to real work. I still treat the output as a draft to verify, not gospel; the judgment stays mine.
